original_hypothesis.title: Unbounded call cost in Tokenomics.trackServiceDonations() (external registry queries per service/unit) enables griefing against Treasury-triggered donation tracking by choosing services with extreme unit counts
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: trackServiceDonations loops: services -> unitTypes(2) -> units(numServiceUnits) and performs multiple external calls (getUnitIdsOfService, ownerOf for service/unit owners, ve votes checks). Although only Treasury can call trackServiceDonations, an attacker can influence which services donations are attributed to (depending on Treasury deposit workflow) and select services with very large unit lists, inflating gas and potentially causing Treasury donation-processing transactions to revert or become too costly, delaying reward accounting for an epoch.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: This is an ops-facing DoS: if Treasury has any permissionless donation intake that later batches tracking on-chain, an attacker can craft inputs (services with high unit counts) to exceed gas limits.
deployment_assumptions: Treasury aggregates/relays donation attribution inputs that an unprivileged attacker can influence
ServiceRegistry permits services with very large component/agent sets
Treasury uses batch processing on-chain within fixed gas limits
original_hypothesis.config_evidence: No Treasury source provided; Tokenomics.trackServiceDonations explicitly trusts Treasury for validation but performs heavy loops and external calls.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Nested loops over services and units; external calls to ServiceRegistry.getUnitIdsOfService and registries.ownerOf inside loops

preconditions: Attacker can cause Treasury to call trackServiceDonations including attacker-chosen serviceIds
Chosen services have large numServiceUnits values

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Heavy per-unit external calls in a single transaction
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Requires Treasury intake design to be attacker-influenced

operational_questions: Can an unprivileged donor choose arbitrary serviceIds for donation attribution?
Does Treasury ever call trackServiceDonations with many services per tx?
What are realistic upper bounds on getUnitIdsOfService return sizes?
validation_requirements: Review Treasury donation intake and batching strategy
Benchmark gas vs number of services/units on a fork
Add fuzz tests with maximal unit arrays

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Create or identify a service with a very large number of units
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Donate in a way that causes Treasury to attribute donations to that service
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Force Treasury’s trackServiceDonations call to exceed gas and revert repeatedly
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Delay epoch incentive accounting and downstream claims

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Donation accounting delayed; incentives not tracked until successful processing
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Medium: Treasury may need to split batches or introduce caps

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Repeated reverts of Treasury donation-processing txs; high gas usage spikes
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Backlog of untracked donations; mismatch between received ETH and accounted totalDonationsETH updates
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Donations repeatedly targeting same high-unit service

validation_result.impact_summary: Filtered before validation: attack_realism is LOW
validation_result.detailed_analysis: This hypothesis was auto-rejected because its attack_realism grade is LOW. Preconditions require actions outside the attacker's control (off-chain compromise, victim cooperation, or admin collusion). No LLM validation was performed.
